ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for imitation crab / surimi stick; SNOMED CT coverage for processed seafood analogues is limited.

main_ingredients: surimi (minced white fish, typically Alaska pollock)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of crab sticks is approximately 3 pieces (90 g), commonly consumed as part of sushi rolls, salads, or as a snack.

nutrient_source.title: Crab Stick (Imitation Crab Meat) - Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate), sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for crab stick / imitation crab meat. Secondary nutrients (sugar, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C) were cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central entry 174202 (Imitation crab, made from surimi) and retained where consistent with approved sources. Trans fat confirmed as 0.0 g per USDA FoodData Central.

gi_source.title: Surimi / imitation crab — analogous estimate based on mixed fish and starch product; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for crab stick / surimi stick found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on the mixed composition of surimi (high-protein fish paste) and starch binders. Fish-based protein products with starch additions typically fall in the GI 35–50 range. Glycemic load per 90 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (40 × 11.07) / 100 ≈ 4.4. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement if one becomes available.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (683 mg per 100 g) from surimi processing and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Crab sticks contain 683 mg sodium per 100 g, well above the 600 mg/100 g threshold; individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit intake to no more than 20–30 g per occasion and must not add soy sauce or other sodium-containing condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (12.3 g/100 g) with low sugar (3.33 g/100 g) and high protein (11.2 g/100 g) moderating glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Crab sticks have a low estimated GI (~40) and moderate carbohydrate load; the high protein content slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glucose rise, making a small portion (≤90 g) manageable for most people with Type 2 Diabetes, provided sweet dipping sauces are avoided and the very high sodium content is factored into overall dietary planning.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high sodium (683 mg/100 g) and ultra-processed formulation with minimal omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Despite low total fat (0.3 g/100 g) and low saturated fat (0.11 g/100 g), the very high sodium content (683 mg/100 g) and ultra-processed nature of surimi-based crab sticks make them unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; whole fish such as salmon or sardines are strongly preferred for cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high sodium (683 mg/100 g) worsens tissue oedema; surimi processing eliminates meaningful omega-3 content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Crab sticks are unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to very high sodium (683 mg/100 g) that exacerbates joint swelling, and the absence of meaningful anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids following surimi processing; whole oily fish (salmon, sardine, mackerel) should be chosen instead.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crab stick, also known as imitation crab meat or kanikama (カニカマ), is a processed seafood product made primarily from surimi — finely minced white fish (typically Alaska pollock) that is washed, blended with starch, egg white, salt, sugar, and crab flavouring, then extruded and steamed into a firm, slightly chewy stick with a red-orange exterior mimicking real crab leg meat. Originating in Japan in the 1970s, crab sticks are now consumed globally and are a common ingredient in California rolls, sushi platters, seafood salads, and snack platters. Per 100 g, crab sticks provide 97 kcal, 11.2 g protein, 0.3 g fat, and 12.3 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 683 mg — a direct consequence of the surimi manufacturing process. Calcium content is relatively high at 131 mg/100 g. The product is ultra-processed and contains wheat starch, making it unsuitable for those with gluten intolerance. Clinically, the very high sodium content makes crab sticks strongly unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For people with Type 2 Diabetes, the low estimated glycaemic index (~40) and high protein content make small portions manageable, but sweet dipping sauces must be avoided. Surimi processing removes most omega-3 fatty acids present in whole fish, eliminating the anti-inflammatory benefit associated with seafood consumption.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in crab stick?
Is imitation crab the same as real crab?
How many calories in crab stick?
Can I eat crab stick if I have high blood pressure?

ingredient_synonyms.kani: Japanese term for crab; colloquially used for crab stick
ingredient_synonyms.kanikama: Japanese abbreviation for kani-kamaboko (crab-flavoured fish cake)
ingredient_synonyms.imitation crab: surimi-based product shaped and flavoured to resemble crab meat
ingredient_synonyms.seafood stick: generic term for surimi-based extruded fish product

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95. Secondary nutrients (sugar, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central entry 174202 and retained as consistent. GI is an analogous estimate (low confidence for GI only) documented with peer-reviewed basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for crab stick / surimi not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database; USDA FoodData Central entry 174202 reports only total fat. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for crab stick and are not consistently available in USDA FoodData Central entry 174202 for this specific product formulation. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central entry 174202 (Imitation crab, made from surimi). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per HPB and USDA sources for surimi-based products. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for crab stick found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. Halal status noted as true based on typical surimi formulations using fish and plant-based ingredients; individual product certification should be verified. The input record referenced 'sushi rice component' in health notes — this record covers the crab stick ingredient only, not a complete sushi roll preparation.
